concepts.title: Solubility and Molar Solubility
concepts.conceptBody:
SOLUBILITY, MOLAR SOLUBILITY AND SOLUBILITY PRODUCT

There are two ways to express a substance’s solubility (a) solubility and (b) molar solubility.

(a) The number of grams of solute in one litre of a saturated solution ($\text{g}/\text{litre}$) is termed as solubility.

(b) The number of moles of solute in one litre of a saturated solution (moles / litre) is defined as molar solubility.

Both these definitions refer to the concentration of saturated solutions at some given temperature (usually 25).

Let us consider solubility of any general salt, ${{\text{A}}_{\text{x}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{y}}}$. Let the solubility of this salt be ‘s’ mol per litre.

${{\text{A}}_{\text{x}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{y}}}(\text{s})\rightleftharpoons \underset{xs}{\mathop{\text{x}{{\text{A}}^{\text{y}+}}(\text{aq})}}\,+\underset{ys}{\mathop{\text{y}{{\text{B}}^{\text{x}-}}(\text{aq})}}\,$

For saturated solution (at equilibrium), ${{K}_{eq}}=\frac{{{\left[ {{A}^{y+}} \right]}^{x}}\times {{\left[ {{B}^{X-}} \right]}^{y}}}{\left[ {{A}_{x}}{{B}_{y}} \right]}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{eq }}}\left[ {{\text{A}}_{\text{X}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{y}}} \right]={{\left[ {{\text{A}}^{\text{y}-}} \right]}^{\text{x}}}{{\left[ {{\text{B}}^{\text{x}-}} \right]}^{\text{y}}}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}={{\left[ {{\text{A}}^{\text{y}+}} \right]}^{\text{x}}}{{\left[ {{\text{B}}^{\text{x}-}} \right]}^{\text{y}}}\quad $ (since ${{\text{A}}_{\text{x}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{y}}}$ is a pure solid)

where ${{K}_{sp}}$ is called solubility product . The term on right hand side is called solubility product if the solution is saturated and ionic product if the solution is not saturated (i.e., not at equilibrium).

${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}={{(\text{xs})}^{\text{x}}}\times {{(\text{yx})}^{\text{y}}}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}={{\text{x}}^{\text{x}}}\times {{\text{y}}^{\text{y}}}\times {{\text{s}}^{(\text{x}+\text{y})}}$

It should be noted that if the ionic product is more than ${{K}_{SP}}$, the equilibrium will shift in backward direction and some amount of salt is thrown out of solution (precipitation) and finally an equilibrium is developed in such a way that the ionic product becomes equal to solubility product. If ionic product is less than ${{\text{K}}_{\text{SP}}}$, the solution is unsaturated and more of the salt can be dissolved. If ionic product is equal to ${{\text{K}}_{\text{SP}}}$, the solution is saturated and if ionic product is more than ${{\text{K}}_{\text{SP}}}$, the solution is called super saturated .

From the solubility product principle, we may infer that,

  • When the ionic product of a salt in solution is equal to its solubility product, the solution is saturated and the undissociated salt remains in equilibrium with its ions in the solution.
  • The solubility of a salt in water depends upon other substances (ions) present is water (at that time) and the solubility product is a constant value (at a given temperature) and is independent of presence of any other ion present. Like other equilibrium constants such as: ${{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}},{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}},{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$ & ${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}$, it is also constant at a certain temperature & can change only when temperature changes.
  • The salts like $\text{AgCl}$ are regarded as sparingly soluble salts. Some of them are: $\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, $\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}},\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{PbCr}{{\text{O}}_{4}},\text{PbS},\text{ZnS},\text{Al}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}},\text{C}{{\text{a}}_{3}}{{\left( \text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}},\text{BaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}},\text{CaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}},\text{MnS}$ etc.

${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}\text{ for A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ : $\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\rightleftharpoons 2\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}}+\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}={{\left[ 2\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}} \right]}^{2}}\left[ \text{SO}_{4}^{2-} \right]$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}\text{ for C}{{\text{a}}_{3}}{{\left( \text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}$ : $\text{C}{{\text{a}}_{3}}{{\left( \text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}\rightleftharpoons 3\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}+2\text{PO}_{4}^{3-}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}={{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{a}}^{2+}} \right]}^{3}}{{\left[ \text{PO}_{4}^{3-} \right]}^{2}}$

In solubilities of sparingly soluble salts, we will deal with mainly two types of problems: one to find out soloubility in water if ${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}$ of a salt is given & vice-versa. Secondly, to find out solubility of a salt in presence of other (common) ions in water.

Comparison of solubility product and ionic product

Solubility product

Ionic product

It is the product of the concentration of ions of the electrolyte each raised to the power of their coefficients in the balanced chemical equation in a saturated solution

It is applicable to only saturated solutions

It has a constant value for an electrolyte at a constant temperature

It is the product of the concentration of ions of the electrolyte each raised to the power of their coefficients in the balanced chemical equation in a solution at any concentration.

It is applicable to all types of solutions of any concentration

Its value is not constant and varies with change

in concentration.

concepts.title: Precipitation of Salts
concepts.conceptBody:
Precipitation of Salts:

For a salt (sparingly soluble) when dissolved in water:

at equilibrium (saturation)$\quad {{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}=\left[ {{\text{A}}^{+}} \right]+\left[ {{\text{B}}^{-}} \right]$

When we mix ions or if there be two or more ions in water, we define reaction coefficient (Q), called as ionic-product (IP) in this case, giving the products of ions in water (ions of soluble salts & other common ions).

IP is product of ionic concentration due to ions already present in water or from a salt. It may be & may not be equal to${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}$.

1. If Ionic Product $(IP)>{{K}_{sp}};$ precipitation takes place till I.P. equals ${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}$

2. If Ionic Product $<{{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}$; a precipitate will not be formed and the solution will be unsaturated.

3. If Ionic Product $={{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}$; a precipitate will not form and the solution is saturated in that salt. (Or we can say that solution is at a critical stage, when precipitation just begins, but actually has not occurred yet in real sense).
